# Questions in Livestream 97

Live Stream #97: Certified Genuine by the IAA

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=412" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">6:52</a> | How are cuneiform names normally rendered into Hebrew, and does a form like Teru to Terah fit the pattern? | He said yes, that kind of shift can fit known transliteration patterns. | He explained that cuneiform spellings often use syllabic padding, while Hebrew tends to avoid names ending in a vowel. On that basis, Hebrew could add a final consonant such as he or het when adapting a name, so the consonantal pattern behind Terah can fit a cuneiform form like Teru without requiring the final consonant to belong to the original source name. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=640" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">10:40</a> | Why is Goshen named in Joshua 10 and 11? Is it an error or two places with the same name? | He treated the issue as explainable without forcing a contradiction, likely by geography and naming overlap. | He favored the explanation that there were two places with the same name, noting that reused place names were common in the ancient world. In Joshua 10:41, the movement from Kadesh-barnea and Gaza toward Gibeon places this Goshen in southern Canaan, not northeastern Egypt, so the geography works as a Canaanite toponym rather than a claim that Joshua conquered Egyptian Goshen. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=843" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:03</a> | Was a bed found at Etemenanki with the same dimensions as Og's bed in Deuteronomy 3:11, and what would that mean? | He was not sure about a physical find there, but said comparable ritual bed dimensions are known from cuneiform texts and may support a ritual context for Og's bed. | He said he could not confirm a discovered physical bed at Etemenanki, but pointed to the Esagila tablet, which describes a ritual bed nine cubits long and four cubits wide. Because those dimensions resemble Og's bed, he suggested Og's bed may have been a ritual object associated with divine kingship or procreation rites rather than simply a personal sleeping bed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=1122" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">18:42</a> | Why did Egyptians move away from chariot warfare, and how did that change their military culture? | He said Egypt did not fully abandon chariots until much later; other platforms such as elephants were added, but chariots remained useful for patrol and mobile archery. | He argued that Egypt never willingly or quickly abandoned chariots; they remained useful into the Roman period and were only truly obsolete much later. Other tools such as war elephants supplemented chariots, especially as elevated archery platforms, but chariots still served as mobile archery and patrol vehicles suited to Egyptian terrain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=1348" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">22:28</a> | Why did early New Kingdom pharaohs imitate Middle Kingdom art and values? | He compared it to later cultures using older prestige forms: it was a way of rooting authority in a revered past. | He said Middle Kingdom art and literature represented refinement, style, and high culture to later Egyptians. Early New Kingdom rulers built near surviving Middle Kingdom monuments and deliberately echoed that prestige, much as later English speakers might evoke Shakespearean or King James language to sound classic and authoritative. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=1457" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">24:17</a> | Why did some ancient writers think Egyptians lived unusually long lives? Did nutrition play a role? | He said adult longevity may have looked high because infant mortality skewed averages, and Egypt's comparatively available medical practice likely mattered more than diet. | He said the Egyptian diet was not dramatically superior and was often low in protein, though wheat helped avoid some deficiencies. The impression of long life came partly because adults who survived early childhood could reach their sixties or seventies, while high infant mortality pulled averages down, and he credited Egyptian medical practice, including wound care, surgery, and effective remedies, as a more important factor than nutrition. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=1907" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:47</a> | Did the Late Bronze Age collapse help or hinder the Israelite Exodus, conquest, and settlement? | He said it did not drive the Exodus, but it weakened Egyptian control over Canaan and helped create room for Israelite settlement. | He distinguished the Exodus itself from Israel's later settlement, saying the collapse was not the cause of the Exodus. Its main significance was that Egypt's economy and hegemony deteriorated after Ramses III, weakening Egyptian control over Canaan and making room for Israel to expand into the Judean foothills. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=2258" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:38</a> | Do we have evidence for desks in the ancient world, or were people usually writing on their laps and tablets? | He said writing was usually done without desks in the modern sense because scribes needed mobile writing surfaces such as palettes, papyrus, and wax tablets. | He said Egyptian scribes commonly wrote cross-legged with a wooden palette over their legs, rolling papyrus across it and using brushes and ink. Because scribes had to work in fields, granaries, military settings, and other mobile contexts, portable palettes and reusable wax tablets made more sense than fixed desks. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=2556" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">42:36</a> | Is it wrong for Christians to jail someone who has repented, and how should Christians balance justice and love? | He said justice and love are not mutually exclusive; circumstances, public harm, restitution, and genuine repentance all matter. | He answered that justice, law, grace, and love each have a proper place, so repentance does not automatically erase criminal consequences. He used an example of a repentant pastor who repaid fraud victims to show how private Christian parties might extend mercy, but said public harm and the broader justice system can still require legal consequences. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=3079" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">51:19</a> | What is your opinion of the Dune books? | He said he loves the Dune saga and also likes the Kevin J. Anderson prequel books, though not every Frank Herbert book is equally easy to read. | He described Dune as probably his favorite science fiction series and said he has read the books repeatedly. He likes the ambiguity and interpretive space in Frank Herbert's writing, enjoys the Kevin J. Anderson prequels as well, and also thought the Villeneuve film captured a strong vision of the story. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=3463" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:43</a> | Since Sunday school level theology can be bad, how should the Bible be taught in a way that does not go over children's heads? | He said to start with simple but accurate stories, increase detail as children grow, avoid dumbing the Bible down, and treat it as a true story. | He urged teachers to begin with simple, factual storytelling rather than distorted or silly versions of biblical narratives. As children grow, teachers should add details onto the story framework, keep vocabulary understandable, avoid exaggeration or minimization, and present Scripture as a true story rather than as contemptible or cartoonish material. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=4166" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:09:26</a> | What evidence shows that Egypt's empire was more of a hegemony? | He pointed especially to the Amarna letters, saying Egypt sought tribute and economic benefit more than direct cultural or administrative control. | He said the Amarna letters show Canaanite rulers fighting each other while Egypt mostly stayed out as long as tribute kept coming. Egypt wanted loot and goods to shore up its inflationary economy, not the expense of direct imperial control, cultural export, or heavy administration like later empires pursued. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=4462" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:22</a> | Do Genesis 1:29-30 indicate that all humans and animals were vegetarians before the fall? | He treated vegetarianism as a possible reading or Edenic ideal, but said the text does not give enough information to settle the issue with certainty. | He said the passage can be read as presenting an Edenic or temple ideal in which eating meat would not belong in the sacred precinct. But he also said the text may be describing an ideal rather than every animal's actual diet, and that the scope of the passage and conditions before the fall are not clear enough to be dogmatic. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=4833" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:20:33</a> | What was the average height of an ancient Israelite? | He estimated around five feet two inches, tying short stature mainly to Bronze and Iron Age diet and protein limits. | He estimated the average ancient Israelite at roughly five foot two, which he said was typical for many ancient populations. He tied shorter stature to Bronze and Iron Age diets that were not especially nutritious or protein-rich, while acknowledging that occasional taller individuals did exist. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=4964" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:22:44</a> | How tall were biblical giants? | He said the answer depends partly on textual tradition and cubit assumptions; Goliath might be read around six or nine feet, with "giant" often meaning unusually tall people. | He explained that the Masoretic reading of Goliath at six cubits would be about nine feet using an eighteen-inch cubit, while the Septuagint's four-cubit reading would be about six feet. He also mentioned Egyptian parody texts about very tall Shasu and emphasized that "giant" can refer to unusually tall humans relative to the much shorter average population, not necessarily mythic-sized beings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=5330" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:28:50</a> | If Kaiser argued for a regional flood around the Mediterranean and ancient Near East, how far back would that need to be pushed to be viable? | He said a regional Black Sea or Mediterranean flood would not need to be pushed very far back, roughly around 10,000 years ago, unlike a global-flood model. | He said a regional Mediterranean or Black Sea flood model would not require the same deep chronology as a global flood model. He placed such a regional event roughly around 10,000 years ago or about 8,000 BC, while saying a global-flood proposal would have to be pushed much farther back. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=5445" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:30:45</a> | Are there good, not-too-technical books on reading Isaiah 53 in its ancient Near Eastern context? | He could not name a specific book offhand, but suggested starting with commentaries such as NICOT or Hermeneia. | He said he did not know the secondary literature on Isaiah well enough to recommend a specific accessible book on the spot. As a starting point, he suggested consulting commentaries, naming NICOT and Hermeneia as likely places to begin. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=5624" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:33:44</a> | Can someone argue for modern same-sex relationships if Leviticus 18:22 was written in an ancient Near Eastern context for an ancient audience? | He said the argument does not work unless the command is only for that ancient audience, and he pointed to New Testament passages as a major problem for that reading. | He said the contextual argument would need to prove that Leviticus 18:22 applied only to its ancient audience, and he did not think that can be sustained. He pointed especially to Romans 1, 1 Corinthians 6:9, and 1 Timothy 1:10 as New Testament texts that extend the issue beyond the ancient Near Eastern setting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=5872" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:37:52</a> | What are the meanings of the titles in Isaiah 9:6, such as Everlasting Father, Mighty God, and Prince of Peace? | He said the titles are meaningful in Hebrew, especially "father of eternity," which he distinguished from saying the Son is the Father. | He walked through the Hebrew titles, treating "wonderful counselor" and "mighty God" as fairly direct and reading "Prince of Peace" in terms of shalom as wholeness, soundness, and unity. He focused especially on "father of eternity," arguing that this construct phrase does not mean the Son is God the Father and that traditional English wording can obscure the grammar. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=6407" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:46:47</a> | What extra-biblical evidence is there for Canaanite evil around the conquest? | He said the evidence is limited, but pointed to Amarna conflict, Egyptian testimony about child sacrifice, Ugaritic parallels, and related regional evidence. | He said the evidence is not abundant, but the Amarna letters show violent conflict among Canaanite rulers, Egyptian sources attest child sacrifice, and Ugaritic and Carthaginian evidence give regional parallels for Canaanite-style religion. He cautioned that the sources are biased and indirect, but argued they support the general picture rather than leaving the biblical claims isolated. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=6595" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:49:55</a> | With early alphabets and similarities between Amorites and Hebrews, could Moses have written the original Torah in an alphabetized early West Semitic dialect? | He said yes, because alphabetic scripts for Semitic languages were already well established before the presumed time of Moses. | He said alphabetic scripts are attested very early, including Semitic alphabetic material from Egypt and Sinai before the thirteenth century BC. Because the evidence for Semitic alphabetic writing was already well established by the presumed time of Moses, he said the serious debate today is more about Moses's existence than whether such writing was possible. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=6781" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:53:01</a> | Can you chart out Jonah's journey, including where he was, where Tarshish was, and how the route worked? | He placed Jonah in the northern kingdom, fleeing through Joppa toward Tarshish in Spain, then being thrown overboard early and later going by land toward Nineveh. | He placed Jonah in the northern kingdom of Israel, then described him going to the coastal port of Joppa to board a ship for Tarshish on Spain's Atlantic side. Mediterranean shipping would normally hug the southern coast westward, but Jonah did not get far before the storm, being thrown overboard, returned to the coast, and then apparently traveling by land toward Nineveh. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=7101" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:58:21</a> | Is Ephraim used as another name for the Messiah, and are there other Messianic names like Immanuel? | He did not know a specific case where Ephraim is synonymous with the Messiah, but said Ephraim can have broader saving or representative associations. | He said he did not know a specific text where Ephraim functions as a direct synonym for the Messiah. However, because "messiah" can be used broadly for an anointed deliverer and Ephraim is tied to Joseph's saving role and figures such as Samuel, he could see Ephraim carrying representative or saving associations in a broader sense. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=7377" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:02:57</a> | Can you give a specific example of long ages being used in the Bible to show clan references rather than literal individual lifespans? | He used Exodus 6 and related clan language to argue that the long ages in Moses's genealogy imply abridged clan descent rather than only individual lifespans. | He used Exodus 6, where Levi, Kohath, and Amram have long life spans, alongside Exodus 1's claim that Israel multiplied greatly in Egypt. Because a few individual generations cannot produce a land-filling population from seventy people, and because the Korahite material explicitly uses clan language, he argued the genealogy is abridged and the long ages are tied to clan descent. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=7974" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:12:54</a> | What is your take on the view that Christianity is an effeminate religion because of teachings like turning the other cheek and loving enemies? | He rejected the claim, saying Christianity defines strength as restrained, virtuous power rather than weakness or gratuitous violence. | He said Christianity is not effeminate or weak, but defines strength through restraint, humility, and morally ordered use of power. Turning the other cheek does not remove the duty to defend the weak or act when war or protection is necessary; it rejects gratuitous, selfish, or sinful uses of power. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=8405" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:20:05</a> | Why is God called "he" if God is beyond gender? | He said God has no body or gender in that sense, but chose masculine father-language to describe how he relates to humanity. | He said God created gender and does not have a body or biological gender in the creaturely sense. The masculine pronoun and father language express the relational role God chose for revealing himself to humanity, so the language is analogical and chosen rather than a claim that God is male in a bodily way. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=8671" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:24:31</a> | How were huge Great Pyramid stones transported over long distances and placed so precisely? | He said they were transported mainly by boat during inundation, then finished and fitted in place rather than fully finished before placement. | He said the key was water transport: Egyptians used Nile inundation, ports, canals or water access, and boats capable of carrying stone loads by displacement. After bringing stones close to the pyramid, workers used sledges for the short remaining distance and finished the outer surfaces after placement, which helped achieve close fitting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=9410" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:36:50</a> | What is your view of hell, such as the Lake of Fire, and is it literal or figurative? | He said he views the Lake of Fire as figurative imagery for real eternal conscious torment caused by God's holiness against sin. | He said he does not read the Lake of Fire as a literal lake, but as imagery for real, eternal conscious torment. In his view, God's holy energy, not God's substance located in hell, is experienced by the unrepentant as torment, while resurrected believers will be able to enjoy holiness because their sin nature is removed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=9776" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:42:56</a> | Why are classicists opposed to the idea that Greco-Roman legal traditions had ancient Near Eastern influence? | He attributed much of the resistance to disciplinary bias that treats Greek and Roman civilization as too independent from older Near Eastern sources. | He said some classical disciplines inherited a bias that treats Greece and Rome as the cradle of true civilization and therefore resists recognizing dependence on older Near Eastern traditions. He argued that Greek and Roman writers sometimes acknowledge outside sources themselves, but classicists may still discount that evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=9959" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:45:59</a> | Did Egyptians have sacred animals they would never hunt? | He said no animal was entirely off the hunting list, though some sacred animals may have been hunted and mummified rather than eaten. | He said Egyptians hunted many animals, including lions, crocodiles, hippos, ibises, and other game, and that sacred status did not necessarily protect an animal from being hunted. The distinction he drew was between hunting and eating: some sacred animals, such as crocodiles or ibises, might be killed and mummified rather than consumed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=10399" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:53:19</a> | How did Egyptians deal with dangerous animals like crocodiles and hippos, and were cats more special than dogs? | He said Egyptians hunted dangerous animals extensively; cats and dogs were both important but filled different roles tied to vermin control, hunting, class, and royal display. | He said Egyptians dealt with crocodiles and hippos by hunting them extensively, eventually eliminating hippos from Egypt and leaving crocodiles mainly in Lake Nasser today. Cats were sacred to Bastet and useful for killing vermin, snakes, and pests, while dogs required feeding and were more associated with higher-status hunting and royal companionship, so each animal had a different role rather than one simply being more special. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=10860" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:01:00</a> | Did Egyptians think the firmament was solid, or was it more symbolic to them? | He said Egyptian views treated the sky as physically real in important ways, including as the body of Nut and a route for the solar boat. | He said Egyptian cosmology did give the sky physical reality, including a heavenly river for the solar boat and the sky as the bodily manifestation of the goddess Nut. While other deities such as Shu and Tefnut also fit into the structure, he did not reduce the firmament to mere symbolism. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=11167" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:06:07</a> | Which festival was being observed in John 5: Sukkot, Purim, or something else? | He said Purim was unlikely and, with caveats, leaned toward Hanukkah rather than Sukkot. | He said Purim was unlikely because it was not a major Second Temple festival. Since John 6 soon mentions Passover, he considered Sukkot possible but leaned toward Hanukkah, which was important in the Second Temple period, fit the calendar space better, and carried special resonance under Roman occupation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=11621" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:13:41</a> | Does Psalm 104:27-30 support reading a carnivore diet into Genesis 1? | He said Psalm 104 is a creation poem and may give some warrant, but its chronology is not precise enough to settle pre-fall diet questions. | He said Psalm 104 is definitely creation language and that verses about creatures receiving food, dying, and returning to dust give some warrant for the argument. But because Psalm 104 is poetic and its chronology moves across creation, flood-like, and post-flood imagery such as wine, he said it cannot precisely settle whether Genesis 1 includes carnivory before the fall. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=12199" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:23:19</a> | What do you think of reading Isaiah 53:11 as Zerubbabel and Darius throughout the passage? | He was not persuaded, saying the wider language of Isaiah 53 does not fit Zerubbabel well. | He was skeptical of the proposed reading, especially when testing it against the surrounding passage. In his view, phrases about being despised, bearing griefs, being pierced for transgressions, and having iniquity laid on him do not fit Zerubbabel as governor, so he was not convinced by the interpretation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=12465" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:27:45</a> | What are your thoughts on qeren in Exodus 34:29, and was Moses's face blistered or was he wearing a horned mask? | He rejected both the blistered-skin and horned-mask readings, preferring that Moses was shining or radiating glory after encountering God. | He said qeren can refer to horns, rays, power, glory, or shining within its lexical range, which explains the older horned-Moses tradition. He rejected the blistered-skin view because the text does not present Moses as maimed, and he rejected the horned-mask view because such masks do not fit the relevant Egyptian or early Semitic context; he preferred Moses radiating God's glory. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=13237" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:40:37</a> | Does Genesis 6:3 have a symbolic meaning? | He said it is best read as God giving 120 years before the flood, not as a symbolic meaning or a cap on post-flood human lifespan. | He said the lifespan-cap reading became especially prominent in twentieth-century young-earth creationist interpretation, but he preferred the older reading that the 120 years are the period of divine patience before the flood. He also connected the question to clan-based readings of genealogies, noting that even Noah may be understood in clan terms rather than as a single individual across every stage. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=13599" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:46:39</a> | Where did Egyptian hieroglyphs come from: Egypt or somewhere else? | He said Egyptian hieroglyphs came from Egypt, even though Egypt was not the only culture to use hieroglyphic writing. | He said Egyptian hieroglyphs arose locally in Egypt, but hieroglyphic writing as a type was not unique to Egypt. He compared other systems, including early pictorial forms of cuneiform and Luwian hieroglyphs, and said each appears to have had a local origin, with Egyptian hieroglyphs being the longest-lived and most successful in the ancient Near East. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=13867" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:51:07</a> | What about the newly discovered chamber in the Great Pyramid? | He said the muon or subatomic-particle scanning found a void, and the probed space looks more like a low, unfinished corridor or chamber than a finished room. | He said particle scanning detected a void in the Great Pyramid, and a camera probe showed a low, pitched-roof space more like a long, unfinished corridor than a full chamber. He suggested it may have been an abandoned or incomplete passage or chamber plan, possibly stopped for structural reasons, and said its end remained uncertain from the probe view. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2KzfCJhrZqg?t=14119" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:55:19</a> | Is there anything cool or any artifacts visible inside the newly discovered pyramid chamber? | He said there were no visible artifacts, but the discovery is still exciting because it shows the Great Pyramid may have more internal features to reveal. | He said the camera did not show artifacts and that the interior looked like an unfinished angled corridor, not a stocked room. Even so, he considered the discovery important because it is the first newly identified internal structure in the Great Pyramid since the nineteenth century and shows that the pyramid still has unknown features. |
